per_query.question: A hotel proposal is within 100m of defence land and 30m of railway land. What consultation windows and deemed-objection rule apply before issuing a building permit?
mandatory_components: A hotel proposal is within 100m of defence land
30m of railway land. What consultation windows
deemed-objection rule apply before issuing a building permit
per_query.expected_answer: Before issuing the building permit, the Secretary must consult (in writing) (a) the Defence establishment officer-in-charge when the site is within 100 m, and (b) the Railway Authority when the site is within 30 m of railway track land. Each authority has 30 days from receipt of the consultation letter to respond; objections received within that 30-day window must be considered. If no objection/remarks are received within the stipulated time, the Secretary may presume there is no objection and proceed; however, if an interim reply is received, the Secretary may delay the final decision until final remarks are received.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Because the site is within 100m of Defence property and within 30m of railway boundary, the Secretary must consult the Defence Officer and the Railway Authority in writing before granting permission. Objections received within 30 days must be considered; if final remarks are not received within 30 days, the Secretary may proceed, but (for such sites) work must not commence until the Secretary intimates the applicant regarding objections, if any.
For development/re-development of land within 100 metres of Defence property, the Secretary must consult the Defence Officer; objections, if any, received within 30 days must be considered before issuing permit, and if final remarks are not received within 30 days the Secretary may proceed.
For works within 30 metres from the boundary of railway track land maintained by Railways, the Secretary must consult the Railway Authority in writing before granting permission; objections, if any, received within 30 days must be considered before issuing permit.
If the site is within 100 metres of Defence property and/or within 30 metres of railway boundary (or within a Security Zone), no work shall commence until the Secretary issues an intimation regarding objections, if any, furnished by the concerned authority/officer as per the relevant sub-rule.
If final remarks are not received from the Defence Officer (or Railway Authorities) within 30 days, the Secretary may proceed; however, any objection received within the 30-day window must be duly considered before issuing permit.
The 30-day objection window applies to the Railway Authority consultation for proposals within 30 metres of railway boundary; objections received within that period must be considered before issuing permit.
Even where the Secretary can proceed after the 30-day consultation period, commencement of work at site is barred for sites within 100m/30m until the Secretary’s intimation regarding objections (if any) is issued.

mandatory_components: what happens if no final remarks are received in 30 days

The Defence Officer / Railway Authority has 30 days to reply. If final remarks are not received within 30 days, the Secretary may delay the decision if an interim reply has been received; otherwise, the Secretary presumes there is no objection and proceeds to issue the permit.

The rule also covers the situation where final remarks are not received within 30 days.

Even where consultation is pending, if the site falls within 100 metres (Defence) and/or 30 metres (railway) and/or a Security Zone, work cannot commence until the Secretary’s intimation letter regarding objections (if any) is issued as per the sub-rule.

per_query.question: In a Security Zone, what extra approvals are required for a religious building proposal and when can permit be issued?
mandatory_components: what extra approvals are required for a religious building proposal
per_query.expected_answer: In a notified Security Zone, the Secretary must consult the District Collector before granting permission; the Collector replies after obtaining specific recommendations from the Director General of Police, and any objections/restrictions must be complied with. For a building for religious purpose/worship, prior approval/clearance/permission and concurrence (as applicable) of the District Collector must be obtained and the Manual of Guidelines to Prevent and Control Communal Disturbances/Promote Communal Harmony must be complied with; the permit can be issued only after the Collector’s concurrence is received.

per_query.question: When are Category-II residential and small non-residential projects exempt from permit, and what prior intimation/objection timeline still applies?
mandatory_components: small non-residential projects exempt from permit
what prior intimation/objection timeline still applies
per_query.expected_answer: No building permit is required for: (a) Category-II Village Panchayats, Group A1 residential buildings with total built-up area (existing + proposed) up to 300 sq.m on all floors and max two storeys, and all single-family residential buildings; and (b) Category-II Group A2 and F occupancies (and specified small units like poultry/livestock farms, traditional coir/handloom/cashew/lime kiln/carpentry/smithy units) with total built-up area (existing + proposed) up to 150 sq.m on all floors and max two storeys (excluding religious/worship uses). Even when exempt, the owner must intimate the Secretary at least 10 days before starting; if the Secretary has objections, they must be communicated within 10 days.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Category-II Village Panchayat projects are exempt from permit for (i) Group A1 residential buildings up to 300 sq.m total built-up area (existing + proposed within the plot), limited to two storeys, and all single-family residential buildings; and (ii) certain small non-residential uses under Group A2 and F and specified rural/industrial activities. However, where the site is near Defence/Railway/security zones, prior intimation applies and work cannot start until the Secretary’s intimation regarding objections, with a 30-day objection window for the consulted authority.
If the site is within 100 m of Defence property and/or within 30 m of the railway boundary and/or within any Security Zone, no work shall commence until the Secretary issues a letter of intimation regarding objections (if any) from the concerned authority/officer.
The consulted Defence/Railway authority/officer must furnish a reply within 30 days of receiving the consultation letter; any objection raised within those 30 days must be considered by the Secretary before issuing permit, and if final remarks are not received within 30 days, the rule’s default handling applies.
Treat as permit-exempt: (xiii) Category-II Village Panchayat Group A1 residential buildings up to 300 sq.m total built-up area (existing + proposed) with max two storeys, and all single-family residential buildings; (xiv) Category-II Village Panchayat Group A2 and F occupancies and listed activities (e.g., poultry/livestock farms, traditional coir works, handloom units, cashew processing, lime kilns, carpentry/smithy units).

per_query.question: Under the special chapter for huts and similar small constructions, what replaces permit and what extra restriction applies near defence/railway/security zones?
mandatory_components: Under the special chapter for huts
what extra restriction applies near defence/railway/security zones
per_query.expected_answer: For huts and the other small constructions covered by the special chapter, a permit is replaced by prior intimation: the owner must file the Appendix A2 proforma at least 10 days before commencement. If the site is within 100 m of Defence property and/or within 30 m of the railway boundary and/or within any Security Zone, work must NOT commence until the owner receives the Secretary’s written intimation regarding objections (if any) from the concerned authorities/officers; the Secretary must obtain and communicate those objections immediately.

However, if the site is within 100 m of Defence property, within 30 m of the railway boundary, or within any Security Zone, work cannot commence until the Secretary issues a letter of intimation addressing any objections from the concerned authority.
Notwithstanding anything in the rules, the special chapter’s provisions apply to huts and certain other constructions (including those referenced in Rule 10 item (xii)). This special chapter procedure replaces the usual permit requirements for the covered works.
If the site is within 100 metres of Defence property, within 30 metres of the railway boundary, and/or within any Security Zone, no work shall commence until the Secretary issues a letter of intimation regarding objections, if any, furnished by the concerned authority/officer.
Where final remarks are not received within 30 days from the Defence officer/Railway Authority, objections (if any) raised within that 30 days must be considered by the Secretary before issuing permit; the Secretary may delay the final decision if an interim reply is received.

If an interim reply is received from Defence/Railway, the Secretary may delay the final decision; any objections raised within 30 days must be duly considered before issuing permit.
If within 100 m of Defence property, within 30 m of railway boundary, or within a Security Zone, do not commence work until receiving the Secretary’s letter of intimation addressing objections, if any, from the concerned authority/officer.

per_query.question: For a project with excavation deeper than 1.5m and risky cut geometry, what extra drawings, neighbour protections, concurrence stage, and compensation mechanism are mandated?
mandatory_components: For a project with excavation deeper than 1.5m
per_query.expected_answer: For excavations deeper than 1.5 m where the depth of cutting exceeds the horizontal distance to the plot boundary, the permit application must include sufficient copies of: (i) dimensioned plans and sectional drawings showing levels/depths of cutting, (ii) drawings/specs/details of temporary and permanent protective measures, (iii) drawings/specs/details of slabs/beams/columns/retaining walls etc. at and below ground floor level, and (iv) pile details (if any). The Secretary must supply copies of these details and the permit to adjoining land owners, and must act on written complaints about actual/possible damage. After earthworks/works up to ground level are completed, the applicant must request concurrence; no above-ground construction may proceed until concurrence (Appendix C1) is issued—within 7 days if works are satisfactory and no complaints exist. If complaints are received, the Secretary must refer the matter within 5 days to a Panchayat-level Technical Expert Committee (Secretary as convener, an Assistant Engineer of LSGD engineering wing, and two experts in structural and geotechnical engineering). The Committee assesses damage, may require protective measures, and fixes compensation equal to the restoration cost plus 30% solatium; concurrence is issued only after measures are completed and compensation (and committee expenses) are paid. Committee process is to be completed within 3 weeks.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Concurrence to proceed with construction above ground level must be issued in the format of Appendix C1, and it is to be issued within 7 days. Concurrence is issued by the Secretary only after protective measures are completed to the Committee’s satisfaction and compensation is paid as decided by the Committee.
Concurrence for carrying out remaining works above ground level (as per approved plans) must be issued within 7 days, after verifying protective measures and payment of compensation. Compensation must include actual restoration cost (as decided by the Committee) plus an additional 30% solatium; concurrence is issued by the Secretary.
Concurrence for proceeding with construction above ground level must be issued in the Appendix C1 format, referencing the underlying permit.

per_query.question: If the Secretary delays permit decision and then the Panchayat also delays, when does deemed approval apply and how is the work treated?
mandatory_components: If the Secretary delays permit decision
per_query.expected_answer: If the Secretary fails to grant/refuse approval or permission within 30 days, the applicant can request the Village Panchayat to decide. If the Panchayat does not determine the matter within one month of receiving that written request, approval/permission is deemed granted, and the applicant may proceed (without contravening the Act/rules). Work done under this deemed approval is treated as duly permitted (not requiring regularisation), and the permit must be issued per rules if the work otherwise complies.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Deemed approval applies if the Village Panchayat does not determine whether approval/permission should be given or not within 1 month from the date it receives the applicant’s written request. After deemed approval, the applicant may proceed with the work, but the work must not contravene the Act, the rules, or any bye-laws.
If the Village Panchayat does not, within 1 month from receipt of the written request, determine whether approval/permission should be given, the approval/permission is deemed to have been given. The applicant may proceed to execute the work, but not in contravention of the Act, rules, or bye-laws.
The 1-month deemed-approval clock runs from the date the Village Panchayat receives the written request. Execution under deemed approval is conditional on compliance with the Act, these rules, and bye-laws.
For constructions/land developments involving earthwork excavation deeper than 1.5 metres, the Secretary must follow the special process in Rule 12, including referral to the Technical Expert Committee within 5 days and supplying copies/details to adjoining land owners.
If the project involves excavation deeper than 1.5 metres, the Secretary must refer the matter to the Technical Expert Committee within 5 days and supply required copies/details to adjoining land owners, and then issue permit as laid down in the rules.

per_query.question: What is the validity period of building/development permits, how many extensions are allowed, and what are extension/renewal fees?
mandatory_components: What is the validity period of building/development permits
per_query.expected_answer: Development/building permits are valid for 3 years from issue. The Secretary may grant up to two extensions, each for 3 more years, if applied for within the permit’s valid period; extension fee is 10% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of extension. One renewal (for 3 years) may be granted if applied for within 1 year after expiry; renewal fee is 50% of the permit fee in force at the time of renewal. For pre-2011 permits with different validity, extension/renewal must be arranged so total validity does not exceed 9 years.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Under KPBR 2011, a permit is valid for 1 year from the date of issue and may be renewed/extended for a further 1 year if the application is filed within the permit’s valid period. Extension fee is 10% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of granting extension, and renewal fee is 50% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of renewal.
The permit is valid for 1 year from the date of issue and may be renewed for a further 1 year if an application is submitted within the valid period.
Extension fee is 10% of the development permit fee or building permit fee (as applicable) in force at the time of granting extension; renewal fee is 50% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of renewal.
An application for extension or renewal must be submitted in white paper (typed or written in ink) and must specify the applicant’s name/address, permit number/date, and the stage of development/construction (if commenced).
The renewal/extension application must be submitted within the valid period of the permit; renewal fee is 50% of the permit fee.
File the renewal/extension application within the permit’s valid period to obtain up to 1 additional year of validity.
Submit the extension/renewal application on white paper (typed or written in ink) with applicant details, permit number/date, and stage of work (if commenced).
Pay extension fee equal to 10% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of granting extension, and/or renewal fee equal to 50% of the applicable permit fee in force at the time of renewal.

per_query.question: A plot with active permit is transferred mid-project. What must transferor/transferee do and within what timeline must Secretary decide?
mandatory_components: A plot with active permit is transferred mid-project. What must transferor/transferee do
within what timeline must Secretary decide
per_query.expected_answer: Before completion and before development/occupancy certificate is obtained, the transferor must inform the Secretary of any transfer (whole/part) of property covered by a development/building permit, including transferee details and intent to transfer the permit. The transferee must obtain the Secretary’s written permission before commencing/continuing work, via a request on stamped white paper with ownership and possession documents and a Rs.25 fee. The Secretary must issue the transfer permission within 15 days of receiving the request if satisfied the transfer won’t adversely affect development/construction.
per_query.predicted_answer:
On transfer of a plot with an active development/building permit, the transferee must obtain the Secretary’s written permission to transfer the permit before commencing or continuing work. The Secretary must decide the transfer request within 15 days if satisfied the transfer will not adversely affect the development/construction; in certain cases the Secretary must refer the matter to the Technical Expert Committee within 5 days, the Committee must complete its process within 3 weeks, and the Secretary must issue concurrence within 7 days if satisfied and no written complaint is received.
A transferee of property along with a development/building permit must, before commencing or continuing work, obtain the Secretary’s written permission transferring the permit. The Secretary must issue written permission transferring the permit within 15 days from receipt of the request if convinced the transfer will not adversely affect the development or construction.
In the procedure covered by this rule, the Secretary must refer the matter to the Technical Expert Committee within 5 days; the Committee must complete its process within 3 weeks. If the Secretary is satisfied works are as per permit/rule and no written complaint is received, the Secretary must issue concurrence (Appendix C1) for remaining works above ground level within 7 days.

If either certificate is not issued within 15 days, it is deemed issued and the owner may proceed accordingly. For occupancy, a tolerance for shortfall in mandatory open spaces/yards may be allowed up to 5% of the minimum required or 20 cm, whichever is less.

per_query.question: For a flood-prone CRZ plot with nearby overhead electric lines, what base restrictions and line-clearance values must be checked?
mandatory_components: For a flood-prone CRZ plot with nearby overhead electric lines
per_query.expected_answer: For a flood-prone plot (or slope >45°, unsuitable percolation soil, floodable area in a scheme, sandy beds), development/construction is barred unless the owner proves to the Secretary that the building will not be dangerous/injurious to health, won’t be subject to flooding/erosion, and won’t cause undue public expenditure. If the land is in a Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ), the project is additionally subject to the restrictions in the Government of India CRZ notification under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (as amended). For nearby overhead electric supply lines, construction must maintain minimum clearances (per Table I / Indian Electricity Rules): Low/medium voltage—2.40 m vertical, 1.20 m horizontal; High voltage up to 33kV—3.70 m vertical, 1.85 m horizontal; Extra-high voltage >33kV—3.70 m + 0.30 m per additional 33kV (or part) vertical, and 1.85 m + 0.30 m per additional 33kV (or part) horizontal.
per_query.predicted_answer:
For a flood-prone CRZ plot with nearby overhead electric lines, you must (i) verify whether the plot falls within any prohibited/regulated strip where development is barred, and (ii) ensure the proposal meets the minimum vertical and horizontal clearances from overhead electric lines per KPBR Table I and applicable electricity law/SEB regulations.
No land development/redevelopment or building construction is permitted within the restricted strip under KPBR Rule 26, and overhead electric line clearances must meet the minimum vertical and horizontal distances in Table I based on the line voltage category.
Every tower or ancillary construction must satisfy minimum vertical and horizontal clearance from electrical lines as stipulated in the Indian Electricity Act, 1910, Indian Electricity Rules, 1956 (as amended), and any regulation issued by the State Electricity Board.
Building permit application must include a site plan (scale not less than 1:400) showing plot boundaries and required site details; this is needed to demonstrate compliance with restrictions and line clearances on the plan set.

per_query.question: What are minimum front/rear/side/interior open-space rules up to 10m height, and how do they scale above 10m?
mandatory_components: What are minimum front/rear/side/interior open-space rules up to 10m height
per_query.expected_answer: Up to 10 m building height: minimum front yard is 3 m (mean 3 m, minimum 1.8 m where full depth can’t be maintained), minimum rear yard is 2 m, and side open spaces must be at least 1.2 m on one side (other than front/rear) and at least 1 m on the other side. Habitable rooms not abutting front/rear/side open spaces must abut an interior open space at least 2.5 m wide (1.5 m wide is sufficient for buildings up to 7 m height). For buildings above 10 m height, the minimum open spaces (and minimum inter-block distances/interior open-space width for the 10 m baseline) must be increased by 0.5 m for every additional 3 m height (or part) above 10 m, provided from ground level for the whole building.
per_query.predicted_answer:
For buildings above 10 metres in height, the minimum front/rear/side open spaces, distances between blocks, and interior open-space width applicable up to 10 metres must be increased at 0.5 metre for every 3 metres (or part) of height exceeding 10 metres. The exact base minimums up to 10 metres vary by occupancy/type and are not fully specified in the provided extract.
For buildings above 10 metres in height, the minimum front, rear and side open spaces, the minimum distance between blocks, and the minimum width of interior open space required up to 10 metres must be increased at 0.5 metre for every 3 metres (or part) exceeding 10 metres.
Where land is surrendered for a proposed road, the mandatory open space/setback from the proposed road boundary may be reduced by the surrendered breadth, but must remain at least 3 metres from the proposed road boundary; for heights exceeding 10 metres, the minimum open spaces must still increase at 0.5 metre per every 3 metres (or part) above 10 metres.
If a side open space is only 0.60 metre, no opening other than a ventilator above 2.0 metres from floor level is permitted on that side; rear side must have average depth 1.0 metre with minimum 0.50 metre, and openings are allowed if open space is 0.90 metre.
Where more than one building is proposed on one plot, it suffices to provide the required open spaces from plot boundaries, with open yards between two buildings not less than 2 metres for buildings up to 10 metres height (as stated for the referenced category).
For certain industrial buildings up to 10 metres height meeting specified thresholds, minimum open spaces are prescribed by group (e.g., Group G1: front 7.5 m, sides 3.0 m, rear 7.5 m; Group G2: front 3.0 m, sides 3.0 m, rear 3.0 m); where more than one building is on the same plot, the rule allows sufficiency based on plot-boundary open spaces (as stated).
If building height exceeds 10 metres, increase the applicable minimum front/rear/side open spaces, inter-block distances, and interior open-space width by 0.5 metre for every 3 metres (or part) above 10 metres, on top of the base requirements applicable up to 10 metres.
If relying on road-widening surrender to reduce setback, ensure the remaining setback from the proposed road boundary is at least 3 metres, and still apply the 0.5 m per 3 m height increase for portions of height exceeding 10 metres.

per_query.question: What are street-centerline and street-boundary distance rules, and what special relaxation exists for short cul-de-sacs or very narrow streets?
mandatory_components: what special relaxation exists for short cul-de-sacs
per_query.expected_answer: Minimum distance from the centre line of a street to any building is 4.50 m. Minimum setback from street boundary is: 2 m for buildings up to 7 m height on roads other than NH/SH/District/notified roads; 3 m on NH/SH/District/notified roads; and for buildings over 7 m height, at least 3 m from the boundary of any street/road. Special relaxation: for cul-de-sacs up to 150 m length, pedestrian lanes, or streets under 3 m width, a 1.50 m distance from the plot boundary abutting the street to the building is sufficient for buildings up to 7 m height, irrespective of the centre-line distance.
per_query.predicted_answer:
Street distance controls are measured either from the street centerline (minimum 4.50 m to buildings) or from the plot boundary abutting the street (typically 2.00 m or 1.50 m depending on the rule context and road category). A special relaxation applies for cul-de-sacs up to 150 m length and for pedestrian lanes/streets below 3 m width: for buildings up to 7 m height, a 1.50 m setback from the plot boundary is sufficient regardless of centerline distance.
The minimum distance between the central line of a street and any building (other than a compound wall/fence/outdoor display structure) shall be 4.50 m.
Minimum distance between the plot boundary abutting any street (other than National/State Highways, district roads, and other roads notified by the Grama Panchayat) and the building (excluding compound wall/fence/outdoor display structure) shall be 2.00 m; street alignment/building line/development plan restrictions also apply simultaneously.
Minimum distance between the plot boundary abutting any street (other than National/State Highways, District Roads, and other roads notified by the Panchayat) and the building (excluding compound wall/fence/outdoor display structure) shall be 1.50 m; front yard minimum depth is 1.50 m.
Every street in a layout shall have width not less than 7 m and be motorable; for cul-de-sacs with length not exceeding 150 m, street width shall not be less than 3 m.
For a cul-de-sac not exceeding 150 m length, or pedestrian lanes/streets below 3 m width, it is sufficient if the distance between the plot boundary abutting the street and the building is 1.50 m for buildings up to 7 m height, irrespective of the distance from the street centerline.
Any restriction under street alignment or building line or both, and any development plan/other rules/byelaws, apply simultaneously in addition to the stated plot-boundary setback.

per_query.question: How is maximum building height computed from street width and setback, and what cap applies in Security Zones?

what cap applies in Security Zones
per_query.expected_answer: Maximum building height is limited to: 1.5 × (width of street abutting the plot) + 1.5 × (width of the yard between building and that street). This height can be further increased by 3 m for every additional 0.50 m (or part) of setback from the building line. In a Security Zone, overall height up to the topmost point must not exceed 10 m (or the lower height specified by the District Collector); if an existing building in the Security Zone is already 10 m or more, further vertical extension is not permitted.

Street width for proposal documentation is the width between plot boundaries on opposite sides, and sectional drawings must show relevant ground levels and building heights as part of the submission details.
For cul-de-sacs up to 150 m length or pedestrian lanes/streets below 3 m width, a 1.50 m distance from plot boundary abutting the street to the building is sufficient for buildings up to 7 m height (subject to the rule’s stated context).
Minimum distance from plot boundary abutting any street (other than specified higher-category roads) to the building is 2 m, and any fixed street alignment/building line restrictions and development plan provisions also apply simultaneously.

per_query.expected_answer: Baseline: the minimum clear access width to the building/plot and the access street from the main street must follow Table 3 (varying by storeys, residential single vs multiple unit, non-residential floor-area bands, and whether the occupancy is Group B/C). For buildings above four storeys, the table indicates 8.0 m access width across listed categories.

per_query.question: For Group A1 apartment parking, what rates apply by carpet-area band, how are fractions handled, and what visitor parking add-on is mandatory?
mandatory_components: what rates apply by carpet-area band
what visitor parking add-on is mandatory
per_query.expected_answer: Group A1 apartment/flat car-parking (Table 4A) is based on carpet area per dwelling unit: up to 60 sq.m—1 space per 4 units; >60 to 100 sq.m—1 per 2 units; >100 to 150 sq.m—1 per unit; >150 to 250 sq.m—1.5 per unit; >250 sq.m—2 per unit. Any fraction in the total parking requirement is rounded up to the next whole number. Additionally, 15% of the mandatory off-street parking requirement must be provided as visitors’ parking, earmarked and maintained exclusively for visitors.

Group A1 off-street parking spaces are required per dwelling unit by carpet-area band: up to 60 sq.m: 1 per 4 units; >60–100 sq.m: 1 per 2 units; >100–150 sq.m: 1 per unit; >150–250 sq.m: 1.5 per unit; >250 sq.m: 2 per unit.

per_query.question: For non-A1 occupancies, what Category-II parking relaxation exists, what two-wheeler add-on is mandatory, and when are loading/unloading bays required?
per_query.expected_answer: For occupancies other than Group A1 apartments/flats, Category-II Village Panchayats may provide only 75% of the parking requirement. Loading/unloading bays (each 30 sq.m) are required, in addition to Table 4B parking, for Group F (mercantile/commercial), Group G1 (industrial), Group G2 (small industrial), and Group H (storage) occupancies at the rate of 1 loading bay + 1 unloading bay per 1000 sq.m (or part) of floor area exceeding the first 700 sq.m.

For non-A1 occupancies, Category-II parking relaxation allows a reduction in required car parking subject to providing an additional two-wheeler parking add-on. Loading/unloading bays are required for specified non-A1 occupancy groups once floor area exceeds a stated threshold.
For Group F (Mercantile/Commercial), Group G1 (Industrial), Group G2 (Small industrial) and Group H (Storage), loading and unloading spaces must be provided in addition to Table 4B parking. Each bay must be 30 sq.m, provided within the plot at a rate of 1 bay per 1000 sq.m of floor area (or fraction), calculated on floor area exceeding the first 700 sq.m.
